cataloging_subject.label: Blanchardyn and Eglantine
cataloging_subject.scope_note: William Caxton's c. 1489 Middle English prose translation and adaptation of the long French prose Blancandin et l'Orgueilleuse d'Amours, including Caxton's dedication to Margaret Beaufort, table, and fifty-four-chapter romance. This intellectual work is distinct from its lost French exemplar, the unique incomplete Manchester print, Leon Kellner's composite 1890 edition and 1962 reprint, Thomas Pope Goodwine's 1595 refashioning, the University of Michigan encoding, and the repository XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: Blanchardyn and Eglantine
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous French prose adapter; translated and adapted by William Caxton

notes: The unique print has no original title page; this established title is supported by the table and modern catalogs and editions.
A normalized cataloging form shared with later English adaptations; it does not make Goodwine's 1595 version identical to Caxton's work.

titles.value: Here begynneth the table of the victoryous prynce Blanchardyn, sone of the noble kyng of Fryse, and of Eglantyne Quene of Tormaday
Caxton's Blanchardyn and Eglantine, c. 1489: From Lord Spencer's Unique Imperfect Copy, Completed by the Original French and the Second English Version of 1595

agents.name: William Caxton
notes: c. 1422–1491/1492; translator, adapter, printer, and publisher of the cataloged English work.

notes: 1859–1928; editor of the immediate 1890 EETS edition.

scope.description: Authorship and adaptation of the long French prose source narrative translated by Caxton

notes: This is source-work responsibility; Caxton is responsible for the Middle English translation and adaptation.

scope.description: Middle English version with additions, expansions, altered emphases, and an English dedication
Immediate composite source edition published in 1890 and reprinted in 1962
Transcription of the unique Caxton print with explicitly marked supplied passages
Editorial comparison apparatus across the edition, drawing on BnF Français 24371 and the 1595 Goodwine print

notes: The normalized range expresses the approximation and is not evidence for exact beginning and ending years.
The printing place is not promoted to a separately proven place of translation.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: French and modern English passages in Kellner's edition and the U-M encoding belong to editorial and comparison layers, not to the whole cataloged Caxton work.

form_statements.description: The romance, dedication, and chapter table are prose. Brief embedded verse inscriptions or quotations, together with verse in Kellner's introduction and French comparisons, do not constitute a second substantive whole-work form.

genre_statements.label: Didactic romance
notes: Hudson describes it as pedagogical; Caxton's dedication commends noble histories as instruction in chivalry and constancy.

subject_statements.label: Christian-Muslim conflict and conversion
notes: The label describes the romance's represented conflict and conversion episodes, not the identities of historical peoples or modern readers.

summaries.value: Kept ignorant of warfare despite his royal birth, Blanchardyn learns of chivalry from Trojan images and secretly leaves Frisia to seek adventure. He wins renown in combat and the love of Eglantine, the Proud Lady in Love, but repeated sieges, captures, shipwrecks, political betrayals, and campaigns separate them. With his companion Sadoyne, Blanchardyn rescues family and allies, defeats Alymodes and Subyon, returns to Eglantine, marries her, and becomes king.

The Caxton work consists of a dedication to Margaret Beaufort, a table, and fifty-four prose chapters. Its plot moves through three broad phases: Blanchardyn's chivalric formation and encounter with Eglantine; campaigns, capture, shipwreck, and alliance with Sadoyne; and the paired rescues and final marriages. The unique surviving print loses one internal leaf and everything after signature Miiii, so later editors reconstruct those portions from French and later English sources.

The source XML represents Kellner's edition rather than a pure continuous copy of Caxton's surviving print: early chapters include parallel passages from the 1595 English adaptation, the internal lacuna is supplied from BnF Français 24371, and the ending combines the French witness with Goodwine. Those editorial supplements are lineage layers, not alternate intrinsic forms or languages of the cataloged Caxton work.

extent.description: The Caxton table enumerates fifty-four prose chapters. The repository XML BODY has one dedication, one table, and fifty-two DIV1 chapter containers because chapters V–VII share a container; its 512 L elements include editorial/source-comparison verse and brief embedded inscriptions or quotations, none of which makes the prose romance mixed-form.

content_parts.label: Caxton's dedication to Margaret Beaufort
content_parts.summary: Caxton presents the translated book to Margaret Beaufort, explains that she returned the French book he had sold her and requested an English translation, and commends noble histories as instruction and recreation.

content_parts.label: Table of fifty-four chapters
content_parts.summary: A chapter table naming Blanchardyn and Eglantine and outlining the complete intended narrative, including material now missing from the unique print.

locators.value: image resources page14–page20; page20 is a later handwritten supply

content_parts.label: Romance narrative
content_parts.summary: Fifty-four chapters following Blanchardyn's education, adventures, love of Eglantine, alliance with Sadoyne, campaigns, rescues, marriages, and accession.

locators.value: image resources page24–page203 for the surviving print, ending at sig. Miiii verso
pp. 11–223, with supplied French and 1595 passages explicitly marked

related_works.title: Blancandin et l'Orgueilleuse d'Amours (long French prose version)
notes: Caxton translated a copy of this version; BnF Français 24371 is an extant close witness and Kellner's source for supplied passages, not securely identified here as Caxton's physical exemplar.

related_works.title: Blancandin et l'Orgueilleuse d'Amours (Old French verse romance)
notes: The early-thirteenth-century verse romance is the ancestor of the long prose source, not Caxton's direct copytext.

related_works.title: The Moste Pleasaunt Historye of Blanchardine (1595)
notes: Goodwine's substantial Elizabethan refashioning is later than and adapted from Caxton; Kellner used selected portions to parallel and continue the incomplete print.

evidence.citation: Blanchardyn XML source, FILEDESC and ENCODINGDESC, in the pinned CME submodule.
evidence.summary: The header identifies the U-M item, Kellner's 1890 edition and 1962 reprint, its composite title, electronic production, exclusions, and treatment of editorial corrections and notes.

evidence.citation: Blanchardyn XML source, Caxton dedication, table, and romance body.
evidence.summary: The dedication states that Caxton translated a French book at Margaret Beaufort's request; the table outlines fifty-four chapters; the narrative supports language, form, plot, genre, subjects, and content structure.

evidence.citation: Blanchardyn XML source, editorial declaration and source-boundary notes.
evidence.summary: The declaration explains sequential encoding of originally parallel Caxton and 1595 versions, the French/English parallel at the ending, inserted milestones, corrections, and omitted back matter. Notes mark the internal French supply and the point where Lord Spencer's Caxton copy ends and Goodwine supplies continuation.

evidence.citation: Structural inspection of the Blanchardyn repository XML.
evidence.locator: TEXT/BODY direct DIV1 children and descendant L elements
evidence.summary: The BODY has fifty-four direct DIV1 children: one dedication, one table, and fifty-two chapter containers; the shared container for chapters V–VII explains the difference from the fifty-four chapters listed in the table. The 512 L elements include editorial/source-comparison verse and brief embedded inscriptions or quotations, so they are encoding metrics rather than evidence that the prose romance is mixed-form.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, Blanchardyn item information.
evidence.locator: Title, editor, publication, rights, contents, and entire-text action
evidence.summary: The item page identifies Kellner and Caxton, dates the edition 1890, exposes the complete encoded text and detailed contents, and marks the materials public domain with No Copyright–United States.

evidence.citation: Leon Kellner, ed., Caxton's Blanchardyn and Eglantine, c. 1489, EETS Extra Series 58 (London, 1890).
evidence.locator: title page; contents; introduction pp. cx–cxxvi; text pp. 1–223; appendix pp. 225–234
evidence.summary: The scan confirms the edition's title, editor, date, base copy, use of BnF Français 24371 and Goodwine 1595, source boundaries, transcription practices, fifty-four-chapter structure, and publication details.

evidence.citation: University of Manchester Library, Early Printing Collection, Bookreader 15027 / Incunable 15027.
evidence.locator: Parent Work Title; Reference Number 15027; creator; [c1489]; Westminster imprint; language; dimensions; citations; image rights
evidence.summary: The official item metadata identifies the unique physical print, its date and Westminster imprint, Middle English language, dimensions, Manchester holding, standard bibliographic identifiers, digital capture, and CC BY-NC-SA image terms.

evidence.citation: Visual inspection of the complete Manchester Incunable 15027 IIIF sequence.
evidence.locator: all 210 canvases; especially image resources page13–page24 and page192–page209
evidence.summary: The 210-canvas sequence covers the complete bound object. Caxton's dedication begins at page13, the later handwritten replacement table leaf is page20, the narrative begins at page24, the surviving print ends at page203, and pages204–209 are blank, flyleaf, or binding views.

evidence.citation: Harriet Hudson, ed., William Caxton's Paris and Vienne and Blanchardyn and Eglantine, Middle English Texts Series (Medieval Institute Publications, 2023; digital edition 2024).
evidence.locator: Introduction to Blanchardyn and Eglantine; Origin and Genre; Textual Matters; Witnesses and Source Texts
evidence.summary: The current edition supports the early-thirteenth-century Picard verse ancestry, Burgundian long prose source, Caxton's translation/adaptation and patron, c. 1489 date, prose romance genres, plot, source witnesses, exact modern identifiers, and physical description and lacunae of Manchester Incunable 15027.

evidence.citation: The Huntington, The moste pleasaunt historye of Blanchardine (1595), call number 81464.
evidence.locator: title, publication date, call number, format, and associated people
evidence.summary: The current holding record identifies the 1595 Goodwine/Caxton adaptation used by Kellner, now Huntington call number 81464; the page supplies no public image.

evidence.citation: Cécile Decaix, ‘Thomas Pope Goodwine's Most Pleasant History of Blanchadyne (1595): The Silent Revival of a Text by Caxton,’ Shakespeare en devenir 16 (2022; updated 2026).
evidence.summary: The article distinguishes Caxton's translation from Goodwine's 1595 Elizabethan paraphrase/refashioning, identifies the 1595 and 1597 editions and printers, and confirms that the surviving 1595 copy is the work represented by current catalog records.
The linked lineage record supplies stable entity IDs for the repository XML, U-M encoding, Kellner editions, Caxton and Goodwine prints, French source witness, and access and rights layers.

open_questions.question: Can Caxton's lost French exemplar be identified more precisely, and was it physically identical with any surviving long-prose manuscript rather than merely textually close to BnF Français 24371?
next_steps: Consult Greco's 2002 stemma, Hudson's full textual notes, and provenance studies of the long prose manuscripts.
Preserve the lost exemplar and BnF Français 24371 as separate entities unless codicological provenance proves identity.

open_questions.question: Can Caxton's translation and printing be dated more narrowly than c. 1489?
next_steps: Review current ISTC/GW dating arguments, paper and type evidence, and the chronology of Margaret Beaufort's commission.
Do not convert the approximate print date into an exact composition date without new evidence.

open_questions.question: How much of Caxton's exact wording can be reconstructed for the missing internal leaf and lost ending, as distinct from reconstructing the narrative of his French source?
next_steps: Compare Kellner's French and Goodwine supplies with Hudson's new English translation of BnF Français 24371 and her textual notes.
Keep supplied text visibly scoped in future editions rather than presenting it as surviving Caxton wording.

notes: The XML's LANGUSAGE lists French and Latin but omits Middle English; direct inspection of the Caxton text, Manchester catalog, and modern edition establishes Middle English as the cataloged work's primary language.

The repository XML's 419 PB milestones, 512 L elements, sixty total DIV1 elements, and editorial front/back matter describe Kellner's composite volume and encoding, not the intrinsic extent or mixed form of Caxton's romance.
Dates, rights, and access conditions for the 1489 print, 1595 print, 1890 edition, 1962 reprint, modern digital editions, and manuscript images remain attached to those separate lineage layers.
